elements.sent-2411-003: The disperse structure distributions (discreteness) and variant scattering characteristics (variability) of SAR airplane targets lead to special challenges of object detection and recognition.
elements.sent-2411-004: The current deep learning-based detectors encounter challenges in distinguishing fine-grained SAR airplanes against complex backgrounds.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address it, we propose a novel physics-guided detector (PGD) learning paradigm for SAR airplanes that comprehensively investigate their discreteness and variability to improve the detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-006: It is a general learning paradigm that can be extended to different existing deep learning-based detectors with "backbone-neck-head" architectures.
elements.sent-2411-007: The main contributions of PGD include the physics-guided self-supervised learning, feature enhancement, and instance perception, denoted as PGSSL, PGFE, and PGIP, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-008: PGSSL aims to construct a self-supervised learning task based on a wide range of SAR airplane targets that encodes the prior knowledge of various discrete structure distributions into the embedded space.
elements.sent-2411-009: Then, PGFE enhances the multi-scale feature representation of a detector, guided by the physics-aware information learned from PGSSL.
elements.sent-2411-010: PGIP is constructed at the detection head to learn the refined and dominant scattering point of each SAR airplane instance, thus alleviating the interference from the complex background.
elements.sent-2411-011: We propose two implementations, denoted as PGD and PGD-Lite, and apply them to various existing detectors with different backbones and detection heads.
elements.sent-2411-012: The experiments demonstrate the flexibility and effectiveness of the proposed PGD, which can improve existing detectors on SAR airplane detection with fine-grained classification task (an improvement of 3.1\% mAP most), and achieve the state-of-the-art performance (90.7\% mAP) on SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-014: SAR target detection and recognition is a fundamental task in this community, and the most concerned targets include ships, vehicles, and airplanes \cite{gao2023onboard .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Most ship and vehicle targets are with rectangle shapes, and therefore they depict relatively more stable and continuous scattering characteristics in SAR images than airplanes \cite{zhao2020pyramid,huang2024scattering , as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:intro .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: SAR airplane targets have specific characteristics, which can be summarized as discreteness and variability.
elements.sent-2411-017: The irregular geometry of airplanes introduces more complex scattering characteristics, such as edge diffraction, multiple scattering, cavity scattering, and reflection.
elements.sent-2411-018: The reflection appeared at the smooth surface of the fuselage will result in weak backscattering intensity compared with wings or engine \cite{suo2024adaptive,kang2021sfr .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In addition, the scatterings of SAR airplane targets are sparse in the complex scene, and the facilities of the airport terminals have stronger backscattering than airplanes.
elements.sent-2411-020: As a result, SAR airplane targets demonstrate more disperse structures and dramatic variations.
elements.sent-2411-021: These lead to more challenges for detection and recognition.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{fig_intro.png} \caption{(a) SAR ship and vehicle targets depict continuous scattering characteristics and regular shapes. (b) As a comparison, SAR airplanes are more discrete and variable.} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-022: Data-driven deep learning-based detectors have achieved remarkable accomplishments, and they mostly require sufficient data for training\cite{gong2023edge,wang2022dense,wang2023krrnet .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Previous studies have made some progress in the field of optical remote sensing\cite{wu2024retentive,wang2024dpmnet .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Compared with optical remote sensing object detection datasets, however, SAR airplane data are limited and more versatile according to different sensors and observation parameters.
elements.sent-2411-025: It will cause severe overfitting and the complex scene at the airport terminals would significantly influence the detection result.
elements.sent-2411-026: Recently, some studies have addressed the above challenges by enhancing the scattering features or emphasizing the relations of discrete structures of SAR airplanes \cite{kang2021sfr,guo2020scattering,suo2024adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-027: These previous methods effectively improved the detection result with better discrimination ability for SAR airplane targets and background.
elements.sent-2411-028: The fine-grained SAR airplane target recognition, on the other hand, is also challenging \cite{zhao2023classification .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The discreteness and variability of SAR airplane targets result in the inter-class similarity and intra-class diversity, which become the main obstacles for fine-grained SAR airplane recognition \cite{kang2023st,sun2022scan .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Some recent work proposed the contrastive learning to address the issue \cite{zhao2023classification .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The recent release of SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset \cite{zhirui2023sar provided a new benchmark that highlighted the main challenges of discreteness and variability for SAR airplane detection and fine-grained classification.}
elements.sent-2411-032: To address the challenges in this task, the mainstream methods mostly focus on designing new model architectures and proposing optimizing methods on the basis of the current deep detectors in the field.
elements.sent-2411-033: The performance reported in the recent literature demonstrated that the performances of the existing detectors still have the room for improvement \cite{zhou2024diffdet4sar,yang2024information,li2024unleashing .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Instead of proposing a specific detection model for SAR airplane targets, this paper aims to explore a novel general learning paradigm that can improve the existing deep learning-based detectors in perceiving the discreteness and variability of SAR airplanes.
elements.sent-2411-035: We observed that the traditional end-to-end learning paradigm has two main obstacles on SAR airplane detection task.
elements.sent-2411-036: The first lies in over-learning the complex scenes to activate them with strong responses in the feature representation while suppressing the airplane targets.
elements.sent-2411-037: The second is to under-estimate the discrete scattering details of SAR airplane instances to result in biased recognition.
elements.sent-2411-038: To this end, our motivation is to excavate the implicit prior knowledge that can provide robust and generalized representations for SAR airplanes, which guide the detector to locate the potential airplane targets more effectively in the complex scene and pay attention to the discrete scattering details for inter-class discrimination.
elements.sent-2411-039: To achieve this, we intuitively have to solving the following concerns: What implicit prior knowledge can we learn from SAR airplanes? How to leverage them to guide the detector learning? Recently, the physics-guided and injected learning paradigm for SAR image classification was proposed \cite{huang2022physically,datcu2023explainable,huang2022progress , and has been also applied to SAR target recognition to achieve remarkable performance \cite{feng2022electromagnetic .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The main concern is to construct a self-supervised learning model for SAR images, where the pretext task is guided with the physical scattering characteristics of SAR.
elements.sent-2411-041: It can be realized with abundant SAR images without manual annotations, and the learned physics-aware features are capable of perceiving the physical properties of SAR.
elements.sent-2411-042: Inspired by them, we propose the physics-guided detector (PGD) learning paradigm for SAR airplane targets in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the previous literature, the polarimetric scattering mechanisms and attributed scattering centers were respectively considered \cite{huang2022physically,feng2022electromagnetic,huang2020hdec .}
elements.sent-2411-044: For airplane targets in single polarization amplitude SAR images, however, they are not applicable.
elements.sent-2411-045: As a result, we decide to use the discrete scattering details of SAR airplanes and their structure distribution characteristics from the amplitude images as the implicit knowledge to guide the detection model for learning a good representation.
elements.sent-2411-046: The proposed PGD learning paradigm offers three novel components compared to existing mainstream detectors.
elements.sent-2411-047: First, we propose the Physics-Guided Self-Supervised Learning (PGSSL) to construct a surrogate task for predicting the scattering distribution of arbitrary SAR airplane targets gathered from diverse sources.
elements.sent-2411-048: The PGSSL model, once pre-trained, effectively captures the discrete scattering characteristics of various SAR airplane targets, embedded in its physics-aware features.
elements.sent-2411-049: The crucial contributing factor is to perceive the different sizes of local scattering relationships mutually within a SAR airplane target.
elements.sent-2411-050: To achieve this, we propose two different implementations based on CNN and Transformer architecture, respectively, for PGSSL.
elements.sent-2411-051: Second, the Physics-Guided Feature Enhancement (PGFE) module is developed to leverage the physics-aware features of PGSSL, enhancing the multi-scale features within the original detector.
elements.sent-2411-052: Finally, the Physics-Guided Instance Perception (PGIP) module is implemented at the detection head to learn more refined representations, facilitating fine-grained detection.
elements.sent-2411-053: We provide two implementations, denoted as PGD and PGD-Lite, respectively, emphasizing better detection results and faster inference speed.
elements.sent-2411-054: We apply them to different existing deep learning-based detectors to demonstrate the effectiveness of PGD.
elements.sent-2411-055: All experiments are conducted on SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-056: The contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-057: A general physics-guided detector (PGD) learning paradigm is proposed for SAR airplane detection and fine-grained classification, aiming to address the challenges of discreteness and variability.
elements.sent-2411-058: It can be extended to different deep learning-based detectors and applicable for various types of backbone models and detection heads.
elements.sent-2411-059: Several implementations, denoted as PGD and PGD-Lite, are provided to illustrate its flexibility and effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-060: PGD is consist of physics-guided self-supervised learning (PGSSL), feature enhancement (PGFE) and instance perception (PGIP).
elements.sent-2411-061: PGSSL and PGFE aim to learn from airplane scattering structure distributions and further facilitate more discriminative feature representations.
elements.sent-2411-062: PGIP is designed to concern the refined scattering characteristics of each instance adaptively at the detection head.
elements.sent-2411-063: The discrete and variable scattering information of SAR airplanes are comprehensively investigated.
elements.sent-2411-064: Extensive experiments are conducted on SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-065: Based on existing detectors, we construct nine PGD models for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-066: The results show that the proposed PGD can achieve the state-of-the-art performance on SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset (90.7\% mAP) compared with other methods, and improve the existing detectors by 3.1\% mAP most.
elements.sent-2411-067: The effectiveness also proves the model-agnostic ability of PGD learning paradigm, showing significant potentials of its utility.

elements.sent-2411-121: The implementation of the proposed PGSSL is given in Fig. \ref{fig:pgssl .}
elements.sent-2411-122: The surrogate task is defined as predicting the scattering structure distribution $S_{\mathrm{dist }$ for each sample $x_\mathrm{plane}$ in the collected SAR airplane targets dataset $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{plane }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: We first extract the dominant scattering points of the airplane target via Harris-Laplace corner detection to obtain the collection of scattering points, denoted as $S_{\mathrm{point }$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Then, the scattering structure distribution $S_{\mathrm{dist }$ is estimated with a Gaussian mixture model \cite{guo2020scattering , which can be optimized by expectation maximization (EM) method.}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Thus, the scattering structure distribution can be represented as: \begin{equation}p(X|\alpha,\mu,\Sigma)=\sum\limits_{k=1}^{K}\alpha_k\cdot{N(X|\bm{\mu_k},\Sigma_k)}\end{equation} where $K$ denotes the pre-defined number of the Gaussian distributions.
elements.sent-2411-126: As shown in Fig. \ref{fig:pgssl (Left), the distribution heatmap $S_{\mathrm{heat }$ can be generated with the estimated statistical parameters $\bm{\mu_k}$, $\Sigma_k$, and $\alpha_k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: We first sort the K distributions according to the weight coefficients $\alpha_k$ in order to determine the importance for each scattering cluster.
elements.sent-2411-128: For each sorted Gaussian distribution, the heatmap $S_{\mathrm{heat ^k$ is generated with $\mathcal{N (\bm{\mu_k ,\Sigma_k)$, where the regions outside the three-sigma are set to 0.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: Thus, the final prediction of the surrogate task is the $\mathit{K}$-channel heatmap $S_{\mathrm{heat }$, for each channel the heatmap values are between 0 and 1.}
elements.sent-2411-130: It is notably that some SAR airplane targets would have limited scattering points in the Gaussian distribution, resulting the estimated parameters unreliable to describe the true statistics information.
elements.sent-2411-131: In this case, the generated heatmap based on the estimated statistic parameters becomes unrealistic.
elements.sent-2411-132: Consequently, we consider the cluster with limited scattering points (e.g., less than 4) as a singular distribution and define a standard normal distribution to represent it.
elements.sent-2411-133: The position of the dominate scattering point in the singular distribution is set as the distribution center, and the corresponding covariance matrix $\Sigma_0$ is set to a relatively small value, i.e., $\left( \begin{smallmatrix 2 0 0 2 \end{smallmatrix \right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-134: We demonstrate that the threshold of the singular distribution and its variance value are two insensitive hyperparameters in this method, and we empirically set them in the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-135: To summarize, the structure distribution heatmap group $S_{\mathrm{heat }$ is obtained from: \begin{equation}S_{\mathrm{heat}}^k(i,j)=\left\{ \begin{aligned} e^{-\frac{1}{2}([i,j] - \bm{\mu_{k}})^{T}\Sigma_{k}^{-1}([i,j] - \bm{\mu_{k}})}, Cnt^k \geq 4, e^{-\frac{1}{2}([i,j] - \bm{\mu_0})^{T}\Sigma_{0}^{-1}([i,j] - \bm{\mu_{0}})}, Cnt^k < 4. \end{aligned} \right.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-160: Then, $F_\mathrm{N ^i$ and $F_\mathrm{P}$ are compressed in advance by a parallel structure with average pooling and max-pooling to obtain query (Q), key (K), and value (V), respectively, where the trade-off parameter $\lambda$ is learnable during training.}
elements.sent-2411-161: In the following, the cross-attention, linear mapping, and feed-forward processing are successively conducted to obtain the output of PGFE, denoted as $F_\mathrm{E ^i$.}
elements.sent-2411-162: To summarize, PGFE can be written as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} V, K = \lambda \cdot \mathrm{AvgP}(F_\mathrm{N}^i) + (1-\lambda) \cdot \mathrm{MaxP}(F_\mathrm{N}^i), F_\mathrm{P}^i = \mathrm{ReSample}(F_\mathrm{P}), Q = \lambda \cdot \mathrm{AvgP}(F_\mathrm{P}^i) + (1-\lambda) \cdot \mathrm{MaxP}(F_\mathrm{P}^i), Z_{\mathrm{att}}=\mathrm{Softmax}\left ( \frac{QK^{T} }{\sqrt{d_\mathrm{K}} } \right ) \cdot V, Z_{\mathrm{temp}} = \beta \cdot \mathrm{Linear}(Z_{\mathrm{att}}) + \alpha \cdot F_\mathrm{N}^i, F_\mathrm{E}^i = \delta \cdot \mathrm{FFN}(Z_{\mathrm{temp}}) + \gamma \cdot Z_{\mathrm{temp}}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\lambda$, $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ are all learnable parameters.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation} V, K = \lambda \cdot AvgP(F_N^i) + (1-\lambda) \cdot MaxP(F_N^i), F_P^i = ReSample(F_P), Q = \lambda \cdot AvgP(F_P^i) + (1-\lambda) \cdot MaxP(F_P^i), Z_{att}=Softmax\left ( \frac{QK^{T} }{\sqrt{d_K} } \right ) \cdot V, Z_{temp} = \beta \cdot Linear(Z_{att}) + \alpha \cdot F_N^i, F_E^i = \delta \cdot FFN(Z_{temp}) + \gamma \cdot Z_{temp}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-163: PGIP is designed at the detection head acting together with classification and regression losses.
elements.sent-2411-164: It is well-known that instance segmentation can substantially improve the detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-165: However, obtaining an accurate and refined instance segmentation map for a SAR airplane with a discrete structure is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-166: Several studies have suggested using the bounding box annotation to create a preliminary semantic map.
elements.sent-2411-167: However, this approach is not appropriate for SAR airplane targets that exhibit sparsity within the bounding box.
elements.sent-2411-168: We will demonstrate the issue in the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-169: In this work, we propose an adaptive instance perception method to generate the discrete semantic supervision of SAR airplanes, that constrains the detection head to learn more discriminative and precise features.
elements.sent-2411-170: Assume that each SAR image $x$ in the training set contains $m$ annotated airplane targets, and the scattering point set $S_{\mathrm{point ^j$ for the $j$-$\mathrm{th}$ annotated target can be obtained.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: We denote the additional sub-network at the detection head as $f_{\mathrm{PGIP }$, that takes $F_\mathrm{E ^i$ as the input and outputs the single-channel prediction map $\hat{L _i$ with a downsampled ratio $d_i$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Simultaneously, the scattering point set $S_{\mathrm{point ^j$ is also down-sampled with a ratio of $d_i$, denoted as $S_{\mathrm{point ^{ij}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-173: The proposed adaptive instance perception aims to predict the locations of the dominant scattering points for each airplane target, while ignoring the other secondary scattering locations that would impair the main topology of SAR airplane target after downsampling.
elements.sent-2411-174: Yet, we set a threshold parameter $\eta$ to decide which scattering points should be preserved during PGIP for each SAR airplane target.
elements.sent-2411-175: The positions where the response values exceed $\eta$ times the maximum response value within $S_{\mathrm{point ^{ij}$ are set to 1, and others are set to 0.}}
elements.sent-2411-176: In details, we denote the PGIP supervision at the $i$-$\mathrm{th}$ detection head as $L_i$, which is obtained from: \begin{equation}\label{equ:Li} L_i(p,q) = \left\{ 1, r(p,q) \ge \eta \max(r_{ij}) 0, others \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Eventually, we can use the focal loss to calculate the objective function between $L_i$ and $\hat{L _i$, denoted as \begin{equation}L_{\mathrm{PGIP}}=\sum_i \mathrm{Focal}(L_i, \hat{L}_i).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-178: The training process of PGD is summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-179: Data preparation: Training set $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{det }$ for SAR airplane target detection.}}
elements.sent-2411-180: For each $x_{\mathrm{det ^i \in \mathcal{D _{\mathrm{det }$ with $m$ annotated targets, obtaining the scattering point set $S_{\mathrm{point ^{ij}$, $j=1,...,m$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Collecting SAR airplane targets as dataset $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{plane }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: For each $x_{\mathrm{plane ^i \in \mathcal{D _{\mathrm{plane }$, obtaining the scattering distribution heatmap $S_{\mathrm{heat ^i$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: PGSSL training: Constructing a PGSSL model $f_{\mathrm{PGN }$ based on a CNN or Transformer architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Training $f_{\mathrm{PGN }$ with $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{plane }$ by optimizing Equation (7) and obtaining $F_\mathrm{P}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Detector training: Appending $f_{\mathrm{PGFE }$ and $f_{\mathrm{PGIP }$ on the existing detector $f_{\mathrm{det }$ to construct the PGD model, where $f_{\mathrm{PGN }$ is frozen during detector training.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: The parameters in $f_{\mathrm{det }$, $f_{\mathrm{PGFE }$, and $f_{\mathrm{PGIP }$ are trained with $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{det }$ by optimizing $L_{\mathrm{cls +L_{\mathrm{reg +L_{\mathrm{PGIP }$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: The additional modules $f_{\mathrm{PGFE }$ and $f_{\mathrm{PGIP }$ only introduce limited learnable parameters.}}

elements.sent-2411-188: We adopt SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset \cite{zhirui2023sar as $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{det }$ for evaluation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: The data were obtained from Gaofen-3 that contain 4,368 images and 16,463 airplane instances, covering seven airplane categories, namely A220, A320/321, A330, ARJ21, Boeing737, Boeing787, and other.
elements.sent-2411-190: There are 3,047 images in the training set, 442 images for validation and 879 images for testing.
elements.sent-2411-191: The image size varies from 800$\times$800 to 1500$\times$1500.
elements.sent-2411-192: In our experiment, the training and testing stages used an identical adjustment of the image size to 1024$\times$1024.
elements.sent-2411-193: At PGSSL stage, we collect different SAR airplane target chips from various data sources to form $\mathcal{D _{\mathrm{plane }$, including SADD \cite{zhang2022sefepnet , TerraSAR-X \cite{pan2016airplane , Multiangle SAR Dataset \cite{ruyi2022multiangle , and SAR-ACD \cite{saracd .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Data augmentation is applied with Gaussian noise, translation, mirroring, rotation, etc, to obtain 10,240 samples in total.
elements.sent-2411-195: The training, validation, and testing sets are split with 7,300, 1,970, and 970, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-196: The input image size is set to 256$\times$192 at PGSSL stage.
elements.sent-2411-197: The PGSSL applies stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimizer with a momentum of 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-198: The initial learning rate is set to 0.001, and it decreases to 10$^{-4}$ and 10$^{-5}$ at the 100$^{th}$ and 150$^{th}$ epoch, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-199: The total training epoch is set to 170.
elements.sent-2411-200: The training of the detector was optimized by SGD with an initial learning rate of 0.005, a momentum of 0.937, and weight decay of 0.0005.
elements.sent-2411-201: The linear Warm-up schedule is utilized to set the learning rate, where the warm-up epoch is 3.
elements.sent-2411-203: The warmup initial bias learning is 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-205: The detector is trained for 300 epochs in total.
elements.sent-2411-206: In the ablation studies, the detector is trained for 50 epochs in total and other parameters are consistent with above settings, considering the convergence speed and calculation time of the models.
elements.sent-2411-207: We conducted all the experiment on a server equipped with four RTX 3090 GPUs while using a single RTX 3090 GPU for inference.
elements.sent-2411-208: We first evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed PGD by constructing different models based on existing detectors.
elements.sent-2411-209: As demonstrated in Table \ref{tab:PGD , we experimented three backbone architectures (ResNet18, ResNet50, and CSPDarkNet) and three detection heads of YOLOv5 \cite{Jocher_YOLOv5_by_Ultralytics_2020 , YOLOv8 \cite{Jocher_Ultralytics_YOLO_2023 , and YOLOv10 \cite{wang2024yolov10 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-210: The detection heads of YOLOv5, YOLOv8, and YOLOv10 are respectively anchor-based, anchor-free, and anchor-free without NMS.
elements.sent-2411-211: For each model, we implement the PGD and PGD-Lite version of it, demonstrating using CNN (HRNet) or Transformer (TransPose) to realize PGSSL respectively.
elements.sent-2411-212: It can be observed that the proposed PGD model improves the mAP of 1.1\%, 1.5\%, and 3.1\% for YOLOv10, YOLOv8, and YOLOv5 on ResNet18, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-213: The mAP of the model has increased by 1.0\%, 2.0\% and 2.5\% respectively on ResNet50.
elements.sent-2411-214: The model's mAP improved by 1.8\%, 1.5\% and 2.2\% on CSPDarkNet, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-215: Among them, PGD-YOLOv5 (ResNet18) achieves the most significant improvement of 3.1\% mAP, and PGD-YOLOv8 (ResNet50) achieves the best mAP of 90.7\%.
elements.sent-2411-216: PGD-Lite denotes the implementation of applying the TransPose architecture for PGSSL with fewer parameters and faster speeds than PGD.
elements.sent-2411-217: PGD-YOLOv5-Lite performs slightly inferior than PGD-YOLOv5, but still achieves significant progress compared with the original YOLOv5 (improving 1.9\%, 2.1\%, and 2.0\% for ResNet18, ResNet50, and CSPDarkNet, respectively).
elements.sent-2411-218: In a word, the results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed PGD learning paradigm integrated with various deep detectors, no matter it is anchor-based or anchor-free, proving its flexibility and model-agnostic characteristic.

elements.sent-2411-219: We compare the proposed PGD-YOLO model with 13 object detection methods on SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset, as illustrated in Table \ref{tab:result .}
elements.sent-2411-220: The compared methods include three advanced query-based transformer detectors (DINO \cite{zhang2023dino , DETReg \cite{bar2022detreg , DEQDet \cite{wang2023deep ), five one-stage detectors (FCOS\cite{tian2020fcos , SuperYOLO \cite{zhang2023superyolo , YOLOv5 \cite{Jocher_YOLOv5_by_Ultralytics_2020 , YOLOv8 \cite{Jocher_Ultralytics_YOLO_2023 , YOLOv10 \cite{wang2024yolov10 ), three two-stage detectors (Faster R-CNN\cite{ren2015faster , Cascade R-CNN \cite{cai2018cascade , RepPoint \cite{yang2019reppoints ), and two detectors for SAR targets (SKG-Net \cite{fu2021scattering , SA-Net \cite{zhirui2023sar ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-221: We use the results of FCOS, YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN, Cascade R-CNN, RepPoint, SKGNet and SANet reported in \cite{li2024unleashing .}
elements.sent-2411-222: The others are implemented by us with the same hyper-parameter settings in this paper (300 epoch training).
elements.sent-2411-223: Among them, the query-based transformer detectors generally outperform the one-stage and two-stage detectors.
elements.sent-2411-225: DINO can achieve the mAP of 85.2\%.
elements.sent-2411-226: Some popular detection methods, such as Faster R-CNN and Cascade R-CNN, only achieve 76.1\% and 75.7\% mAP, respectively, which are inferior than transformer detectors.
elements.sent-2411-227: As a comparison, our YOLO-based PGD model achieves an mAP of 90.7\%, surpassing other existing mainstream detection methods in computer vision field and SAR community.
elements.sent-2411-228: In addition, our model achieves best or second-best mAP in all categories on the SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-229: Specifically, PGD models have remarkable superiority on A220, Boeing737, A330, and ARJ21 categories.

elements.sent-2411-231: In order to assess the soundness of each module in PGD, a sequence of ablation experiments were conducted.
elements.sent-2411-232: The original YOLOv5 employs CSPDarknet and C3 architectures for feature extraction \cite{Jocher_YOLOv5_by_Ultralytics_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-233: It has multiple variants in terms of different depths and widths of backbones, such as YOLOv5-s, YOLOv5-m, YOLOv5-l, YOLOv5-x.
elements.sent-2411-234: We test the detection results as well as the computational cost on these variants, as recorded in Table \ref{table:yolo .}
elements.sent-2411-235: The YOLOv5-x achieves the highest mAP yet being costly.
elements.sent-2411-236: To this end, we optimize the backbone model to alleviate the high cost and reach a good performance based on YOLOv5 architecture.
elements.sent-2411-237: The C3 and CSPDarknet are replaced with a ResNet model with Rep-PAN \cite{li2024yolov , where the multi-scale features are efficiently extracted and integrated to achieve better representation.}
elements.sent-2411-238: As shown in Table \ref{table:yolo , it achieves an mAP of 83.3\% and has fewer parameters than YOLOv5-m.}
elements.sent-2411-239: The modified model is denoted as YOLOv5* and regarded as our baseline model in the following ablation studies.
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{Detection performance of different variants of Yolov5} \label{table:yolo} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{5mm}{ \begin{tabular}{ccccc} Models mAP \#Params(M) YOLOv5s 79.9 7 YOLOv5m 83.0 21 YOLOv5l 84.7 46 YOLOv5x 85.5 87 YOLOv5* 83.3 19 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-240: The ablation studies presented in Table \ref{tab:ablation demonstrate the effectiveness of the PGSSL, PGFE, and PGIP modules.}
elements.sent-2411-241: In these experiments, PGSSL is implemented using the HRNet architecture, with a simple feature concatenation approach applied to isolate the effect of PGSSL, as shown in the second row of the table.
elements.sent-2411-242: The results indicate that integrating PGSSL into the detector significantly improves mAP by 1.5\%.
elements.sent-2411-243: This enhancement suggests that PGSSL effectively boosts the detector’s capability to correctly identify positive instances.
elements.sent-2411-244: The improvement is attributed to the generalizable feature representations learned by PGSSL, which are highly sensitive to the structural distributions of various SAR airplane targets.
elements.sent-2411-245: A comparison between the second and third rows of Table \ref{tab:ablation reveals the impact of the PGFE module.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Incorporating PGFE, which leverages adaptive feature enhancement guided by physics-aware information from PGSSL, further enhances the detector’s discriminative representation abilities, leading to a 4.1\% increase in mAP.
elements.sent-2411-247: This suggests that PGFE effectively complements PGSSL by refining feature representations based on physics-based guidance.
elements.sent-2411-248: The PGIP module, which is integrated at the detection head, also improves the results by imposing a self-adaptive constraint on dominant scattering structures.
elements.sent-2411-249: It helps the detector better distinguish between true positives and false alarms.
elements.sent-2411-250: By adding the PGIP module, the mAP can be further improved by 1.3\%.
elements.sent-2411-251: Overall, the proposed PGD framework achieves substantial performance gains, increasing mAP from 83.3\% to 88.0\%.
elements.sent-2411-252: These results underscore the effectiveness of the integrated modules in enhancing the detection capabilities of the model.

elements.sent-2411-253: We propose two alternatives for realizing PGSSL, that are, HRNet and TransPose, denoted as PGD and PGD-Lite, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-254: They can both improve the performance of the detector.
elements.sent-2411-255: PGD has more advantage in improving the detection results, while PGD-Lite is more lightweight.
elements.sent-2411-256: The results and computational costs for PGD and PGD-Lite are detailed in Table \ref{tab:skpn .}
elements.sent-2411-257: The results indicate that the HRNet-based PGSSL achieves slightly higher mAP than the TransPose-based implementation.
elements.sent-2411-258: However, the TransPose-based approach requires less computational resources, making it more efficient in terms of time and resource usage.
elements.sent-2411-259: Given these factors, while the HRNet-based PGSSL offers a marginal performance advantage, the TransPose-based PGSSL is a more time-efficient and resource-efficient option.

elements.sent-2411-265: The PGFE module is designed to enhance the feature representation of targets for detection by leveraging physics-aware embeddings learned from PGSSL.
elements.sent-2411-266: We experimented with different feature fusion strategies to demonstrate the effectiveness of PGFE, as detailed in Table \ref{tab:caf .}
elements.figure-2411-014: \caption{Comparison of different implementations for physics-guided feature enhancement (PGFE).} \label{tab:caf} \begin{tabular}{cccc} PGFE \#Params(M) GFLOPS mAP AddFusion 48 271.2 84.8 NINFusion 49 272.9 85.9 Ours 50 279.0 87.4 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-267: Initially, we applied the AddFusion strategy, where multi-scale features from the Feature Pyramid Network (FPN) are directly added to the pre-trained physics-aware features from PGSSL.
elements.sent-2411-268: This approach does not introduce additional parameters and achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 84.8\%.
elements.sent-2411-269: Next, we evaluated the NINFusion \cite{DBLP:conf/bmvc/Li0T018 , which concatenates features and processes them using a Network-in-Network architecture \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/LinCY13 .}}
elements.sent-2411-270: Compared to AddFusion, NINFusion improves the mAP by 1.1\%, indicating its effectiveness in better integrating features.
elements.sent-2411-271: However, both of these strategies treat the FPN features and physics-aware (PA) features equally, overlooking their distinct contributions to detection.
elements.sent-2411-272: The PA features from PGSSL are adept at capturing various SAR airplane representations but are less effective at characterizing other targets.
elements.sent-2411-273: In contrast, FPN features from the original detector are tailored to represent complex scenes with diverse scattering patterns.
elements.sent-2411-274: Therefore, a more nuanced approach that considers the complementary nature of these features is necessary.
elements.sent-2411-275: The proposed PGFE employs a cross-attention-based module, where PA features are treated as the query, and FPN features serve as both the value and key.
elements.sent-2411-276: This mechanism re-weights the multi-scale FPN features according to the physics-aware information provided by PGSSL, resulting in features that are more discriminative for target representation while maintaining robust representation for complex scenes.
elements.sent-2411-277: The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed PGFE achieves the highest mAP of 87.4\% and the params only increased by 2M, outperforming other fusion strategies.
elements.sent-2411-278: Additionally, we utilize Grad-CAM to provide a deeper analysis of the various fusion strategies employed for integrating the physics-aware features from PGSSL with the detection model’s features.
elements.sent-2411-279: The corresponding saliency maps are presented in Fig. \ref{fig:pgfe_exp .}
elements.sent-2411-280: These maps highlight the key discriminative features used for target detection.
elements.sent-2411-281: The results reveal that both the simple addition and NIN fusion strategies fail to fully capitalize on the advantages of the pre-trained PA features.
elements.sent-2411-282: These methods do not effectively complement the unique representations provided by PGSSL and the original detection model.
elements.sent-2411-283: In contrast, the proposed PGFE method shows a marked improvement in focusing on the airplane targets, significantly reducing the interference from the surrounding complex scene.
elements.sent-2411-284: This demonstrates the superior capability of PGFE in leveraging physics-aware information to enhance target detection.
elements.sent-2411-285: In the PGIP module at the detection head, we introduce an adaptive instance perception (IP) learning strategy to emphasize the refined, discrete features of SAR airplane targets.
elements.sent-2411-286: We compare this adaptive IP method with two alternative strategies: hard IP and truncated IP, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:IPexp .}
elements.sent-2411-287: In the hard IP approach, semantic supervision $L_i$ is applied uniformly across a rectangular region defined by the annotated bounding box, with all pixels within this box assigned a value of 1 for loss calculation.
elements.sent-2411-288: Conversely, the truncated IP strategy focuses on supervising only the strong scattering points within the image, a technique frequently used in related literature.
elements.sent-2411-289: This method employs a global parameter to filter out strong scattering pixels, but it does not account for the relative variations in backscattering that different SAR airplanes may exhibit.
elements.figure-2411-015: \caption{Comparison of different implementations for physics-guided instance perception (PGIP).} \label{tab:SRL} \begin{tabular}{cccc} PGIP Hard IP Truncated IP Truncated IP mAP 83.9 84.2 84.6 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-290: The visualized results indicate that the hard IP strategy captures only the coarse features of each target, neglecting the specific discrete characteristics that are crucial for accurate detection.
elements.sent-2411-291: Although the truncated IP method can further refine these discrete features, its reliance on a global truncation threshold results in the omission of some important features with relatively weaker backscattering, such as the wings that exhibit primary surface scattering characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-292: In contrast, the proposed adaptive IP approach effectively captures both the discrete and critical local features of SAR airplanes, leading to improved detection performance, as demonstrated in Table \ref{tab:SRL .}
elements.sent-2411-293: In this paper, a novel physics-guided detector (PGD) learning paradigm is proposed for SAR airplanes and it can be extended to various deep learning-based detectors to facilitate the generalization ability and alleviate overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-294: The motivation is to comprehensively leverage the implicit prior knowledge in the discrete and variable structure distributions of a wide range of SAR airplanes obtained from multiple sources.
elements.sent-2411-295: The proposed PGD has three main contributions, including PGSSL, PGFE, and PGIP.
elements.sent-2411-296: PGSSL is constructed as a distribution heatmap prediction task by introducing some external knowledge of various airplane images, which aims to learn the efficient and generalizable representations aware of the structure distribution of SAR airplanes.
elements.sent-2411-297: Subsequently, PGFE is designed as a cross-attention-based fusion module to guide the backbone features of the detector to pay more attention to the discrete structure distributions of targets.
elements.sent-2411-298: PGIP at the detection head constrains the detector from focusing the dominate scattering points rather than the complex background.
elements.sent-2411-299: We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed PGD with extensive experiments on SAR-AIRcraft-1.0 dataset, where PGD is applied to different deep learning-based detectors for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-300: We discussed several implementations of PGD, demonstrating the flexibility and effectiveness of the proposed method.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran .}
